title: Beating noise in frequency estimation with squeezing and memory in continuous-variable systems
authors: Ayan Patra, Manju, Aditi Sen De, Matteo G. A. Paris
abstract: Quantum metrology promises precision beyond classical limits, yet environmental noise typically degrades the quantum resources required for such enhancement. In this work, we investigate frequency estimation in noisy continuous-variable systems, focusing on two complementary strategies to mitigate decoherence: Hamiltonian engineering and the exploitation of non-Markovian dynamics. By embedding squeezing directly into the system Hamiltonian, we show that the quantum Fisher information (QFI) may acquire a tunable higher-order time dependence, leading to enhanced sensitivity in the short-time regime. Moving beyond the Markovian approximation, we employ the quantum Brownian motion model to demonstrate that structured environments with finite memory can induce information backflow, temporarily restoring and even improving estimation precision relative to the unitary limit. We further assess the achievability of these bounds via Gaussian measurements, identifying regimes where homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized general-dyne measurements saturate the QFI, and noting that stronger squeezing widens the gap, potentially requiring non-Gaussian measurement strategies. Our results establish that jointly tailoring system Hamiltonian and environmental memory offers a viable route toward robust quantum-enhanced frequency estimation in open systems.
summary: This paper investigates how squeezing and environmental memory can be used to improve frequency estimation precision in noisy continuous-variable systems. It demonstrates that squeezing can change the fundamental scaling of precision with time and that non-Markovianity can temporarily recover lost information. The work also identifies the limits of Gaussian measurements in high-squeezing regimes.
main_problem: How to mitigate the detrimental effects of environmental noise and decoherence on the precision of frequency estimation in continuous-variable quantum systems.
main_result: The study finds that Hamiltonian engineering via squeezing can enhance QFI scaling to $t^3$ in the short-time regime, while non-Markovian memory effects can induce information backflow to temporarily restore estimation precision; however, strong squeezing may eventually require non-Gaussian measurements to saturate the QFI.
method: The authors use the Gaussian quantum state formalism, calculating Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and Classical Fisher Information (CFI) using the first and second moments of the system's state under various master equations.
model_or_system: A single-mode harmonic oscillator (continuous-variable system) initially in a coherent state, subject to a Hamiltonian that can include squeezing, and interacting with either a Markovian thermal bath or a non-Markovian Quantum Brownian Motion environment.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), and the Symmetric Logarithmic Derivative (SLD).
important_parameters: Frequency ($\omega$), squeezing parameter ($ eta$), damping rate ($\gamma$), thermal photon number ($n$), and the non-Markovianity ratio ($r = \Lambda_c/\omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on the Gaussian regime and Gaussian measurement strategies (homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized dyne).
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows time-dependent dissipation and diffusion coefficients; Figure 3 compares QFI under unitary, non-Markovian, and Markovian dynamics; Figure 5 shows CFI for different dyne measurements; Figure 6 illustrates the growing gap between QFI and CFI with increasing squeezing; Figure 7 shows the failure of fixed quadrature measurements in non-Markovian regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of frequency estimation in noisy CV systems, develops the mathematical framework for Gaussian states under Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics, analyzes the impact of squeezing on QFI scaling, evaluates the role of environmental memory, and concludes by assessing the optimality of various Gaussian measurement strategies.
why_interesting: It provides a dual-strategy approach (Hamiltonian engineering and environmental control) to combat decoherence, which is highly relevant for developing robust quantum sensors and understanding the metrological utility of non-Markovianity in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: QFI as a function of time. In the left panel (a), γ = 0; the black (continuous) and gray (continuous) curves correspond to |β| = 0 and |β| = 0.5, respectively. In the right panel (b), γ = 0.05. Other parameters are fixed at ω = 2.1, β = −0.5i, and α = i.
FIG. 2: (a) Γ+(t) = ∆(t) + γ(t) (ordinate) and (b) Γ+(t) = ∆(t) −γ(t) (ordinate) as a function of time (abscissa). The other parameter values are ω = 7, ξ = 0.3, and Λc = 1.

FIG. 3: QFI (ordinate) against time (abscissa) under unitary, non-Markovian and Markovian dynamics without squeezing (β = 0). The left panel (a) corresponds to Λc = 2 and ω = 1 (Markovian evolution). For right panel (b), Λc = 1 and ω = 7 with ξ = 0.3 and α = 0.1 (Non-Markovian dynamics).

FIG. 4: Competition between Hamiltonian engineering and non-Markovianity in parameter estimation. QFI (ordinate) with respect to time (abscissa) when squeezing is introduced, i.e., β ̸= 0. The parameters are ξ = 0.3, Λc = 1, ω = 5, β = 0.2, α = 0.025, kBT/¯hΛc = 4, γ = 0.07 and n = 0.4. There exists a time region in which F with β ̸= 0 under Markovian dynamics can be higher than that of non-Markovian evoluion without β. It shows that the Hamiltonian engineering can sometime be more beneficial than the non-Markovian dynamics.

FIG. 5: Classical Fisher information (CFI) as a function of time. The CFI corresponding to homodyne and heterodyne measurements are shown in gray (dashed) and black (dot-dashed), respectively, while the dyne-optimal CFI is depicted by the red (continuous) curve. The dyne-optimal CFI coincides with the maximum of the homodyne and heterodyne curves. The parameters are ω = 2.1, γ = 0.05, n = 0.1, and β = 0.3 −0.5i, with α = i.
